**Cyrene**, also sometimes [anglicized](/wiki/Anglicization_of_names "Anglicization of names") as **Kyrene**, was an [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greeks "Ancient Greeks") [colony](/wiki/Greek_colonization "Greek colonization") and [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Romans "Ancient Romans") [city](/wiki/Cities_of_the_Roman_Empire "Cities of the Roman Empire") near present-day [Shahhat](/wiki/Shahhat "Shahhat") in northeastern [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya") in [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"). It was part of the [Pentapolis](/wiki/Pentapolis_(North_Africa) "Pentapolis (North Africa)"), an important group of five cities in the region, and gave the area its classical and early modern name [Cyrenaica](/wiki/Cyrenaica "Cyrenaica").

Cyrene lies on a ridge of the [Jebel Akhdar](/wiki/Jebel_Akhdar_(Libya) "Jebel Akhdar (Libya)") uplands. The archaeological remains cover several hectares and include several monumental temples, stoas, theatres, bathhouses, churches, and palatial residences. The city is surrounded by the [Necropolis of Cyrene](/wiki/Necropolis_of_Cyrene "Necropolis of Cyrene"). Since 1982, it has been a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The city's port was [Apollonia (Marsa Sousa)](/wiki/Apollonia,_Cyrenaica "Apollonia, Cyrenaica"), located about 16 kilometres (10 mi) to the north.

The city was attributed to [Apollo](/wiki/Apollo "Apollo") and the legendary [etymon](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") [Cyrene](/wiki/Cyrene_(mythology) "Cyrene (mythology)") by the Greeks themselves but it was probably actually colonized by settlers from [Thera](/wiki/Thera "Thera") (modern [Santorini](/wiki/Santorini "Santorini")) in the late seventh century BC. It was initially ruled by a dynasty of monarchs called the Battiads, who grew rich and powerful as a result of successive waves of immigration and the export of horses and [silphium](/wiki/Silphium "Silphium"), a medicinal plant. By the fifth century BC, they had expanded their control over the other cities of Cyrenaica. It became the seat of the [Cyrenaics](/wiki/Cyrenaics "Cyrenaics"), a [school of philosophy](/wiki/Greek_philosophy "Greek philosophy") in the fourth century BC, founded by [Aristippus](/wiki/Aristippus "Aristippus"), a disciple of [Socrates](/wiki/Socrates "Socrates"). In the [Hellenistic Age](/wiki/Hellenistic_Age "Hellenistic Age"), the city alternated between being part of [Ptolemaic Egypt](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Egypt "Ptolemaic Egypt") and the capital of an independent kingdom. It was also an important [Jewish](/wiki/Ancient_Judaism "Ancient Judaism") hub. In 96 BC, it passed to the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") and became part of the province of [Crete and Cyrenaica](/wiki/Crete_and_Cyrenaica "Crete and Cyrenaica"). The city was destroyed by Jewish fighters in AD 115 during the [Diaspora revolt](/wiki/Diaspora_revolt "Diaspora revolt"), and slowly rebuilt over the following century. Earthquakes in 262 and 365 devastated the city, but some habitation continued through the early Byzantine period and the [Muslim conquest of the Maghreb](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Maghreb "Muslim conquest of the Maghreb") in 642, after which the site was abandoned until the establishment of an Italian military base on the site in 1913. Excavations have been ongoing since that time.

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

*Cyrene* is the [latinized](/wiki/Latinization_of_names "Latinization of names") form of the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language") name *Kȳrḗnē* (Κυρήνη) of uncertain origin. The Greeks themselves [attributed the name](/wiki/Folk_etymology "Folk etymology") to the legendary [Thessalian](/wiki/Thessaly "Thessaly") princess [Cyrene](/wiki/Cyrene_(mythology) "Cyrene (mythology)") who supposedly founded the city with help from the [sun god](/wiki/Sun_god "Sun god") [Apollo](/wiki/Apollo "Apollo").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013200-2) Some modern scholars sometimes attribute the name to its [spring](/wiki/Spring_(hydrology) "Spring (hydrology)") Cyra (Κύρα, *Kýra*), which was considered sacred to Apollo by the city's Greco-Roman inhabitants.[[3]](#cite_note-janky-3) The legend of Thessalian Cyrene seems to long predate attestation of the spring, however, and [Janko](/wiki/Richard_Janko "Richard Janko") instead suggests that the existing legend and name were adopted by the early Theran settlers for this specific location after some unattested but similar local name in the [Libu](/wiki/Libu "Libu") or [Garamantian](/wiki/Garamantes "Garamantes") language.[[3]](#cite_note-janky-3) Although both the Greek and Latin forms of the name were pronounced something like [/kuˈreɪneɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*koo-RAY-nay*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) they are more often read in English as [/kaɪˈriːni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ky-REE-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") or, in its Latin form, [/saɪˈriːni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*sy-REE-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

People have lived in Cyrenaica since the [Palaeolithic](/wiki/Palaeolithic "Palaeolithic"). There is some evidence for settlement in the caves below the Acropolis which may pre-date Greek settlement. It is possible that [Minoans](/wiki/Minoans "Minoans") and [Mycenaeans](/wiki/Mycenaeans "Mycenaeans") visited Cyrene in the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"), since it is on the easiest sea route from the [Aegean](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea") to Egypt, but the only archaeological evidence for this are separate finds of a small Minoan altar and a Minoan seal, which might have been brought over at a later date.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013200-2)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Foundation")]

Main article: [Greek colonisation](/wiki/Greek_colonisation "Greek colonisation")

A [Greek myth](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology") first recorded by [Pindar](/wiki/Pindar "Pindar") in the early fifth century BC reports that the god [Apollo](/wiki/Apollo "Apollo") fell in love with the huntress [Cyrene](/wiki/Cyrene_(mythology) "Cyrene (mythology)") and brought her to Libya, where she gave birth to a son [Aristaeus](/wiki/Aristaeus "Aristaeus").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013200-2) Greek historical traditions, reported in [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus")' *[Histories](/wiki/Histories_(Herodotus) "Histories (Herodotus)")* and in a fourth-century BC inscription found at Cyrene, say that a group of [Cretan](/wiki/Crete "Crete") Greeks, who had been expelled from [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta") and settled on the island of [Thera](/wiki/Santorini "Santorini"), founded Cyrene in 631 BC, under the leadership of [Battus I](/wiki/Battus_I_of_Cyrene "Battus I of Cyrene"), at the prompting of the [Oracle of Delphi](/wiki/Oracle_of_Delphi "Oracle of Delphi").[[6]](#cite_note-Osborne-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202319-7) Some traditions say that the settlers left Thera because of a famine, others because of a civil war. Most say that the colonists first settled on an island at Aziris (east of [Derna](/wiki/Derna,_Libya "Derna, Libya")) before relocating to Cyrene.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202319-7) The historicity of these narratives is uncertain, particularly the idea that Thera was Cyrene's sole "mother city". Relationships with other cities, such as Sparta[[8]](#cite_note-8) and Samos,[[9]](#cite_note-9) mentioned in the foundation narratives, are uncertain.[[6]](#cite_note-Osborne-6)

Archaeological evidence from the site, especially ceramic finds, confirm that Greek settlement began in the mid-seventh century BC. This early pottery derves from Thera, Sparta, and Samos, but also [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes "Rhodes").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202318-10) The initial area of habitation was a ridge stretching eastwards from the Acropolis to the [Agora](/wiki/Agora "Agora"), but the city rapidly expanded eastwards.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013156-11) The sanctuary of Apollo to the north of the Acropolis, of Demeter to the south, and of Zeus to the east all go back to the seventh or sixth centuries BC. Archaeological evidence shows that several other sites in Cyrenaica, such as Apollonia, [Euesperides](/wiki/Euesperides "Euesperides"), and [Taucheira](/wiki/Taucheira "Taucheira") (modern [Benghazi](/wiki/Benghazi "Benghazi") and [Tocra](/wiki/Tocra "Tocra")) were settled at the same time as Cyrene.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202318-10)

### Archaic period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Archaic period")]

After its foundation, the city was ruled by a series of monarchs descended from Battus I. Over the course of the sixth century BC, Cyrene grew to become the most powerful city in the region.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202321-12) In the first half of the sixth century BC, [Battus II](/wiki/Battus_II_of_Cyrene "Battus II of Cyrene") encouraged further Greek settlement in the city, especially from the [Peloponnese](/wiki/Peloponnese "Peloponnese") and Crete. This sparked conflict with the indigenous Libyans, whose king [Adicran](/w/index.php?title=Adicran&action=edit&redlink=1 "Adicran (page does not exist)") appealed to Egypt for help around 570 BC. The [pharaoh](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") [Apries](/wiki/Apries "Apries") launched a military expedition against Cyrene, but was decisively defeated at the Battle of Irasa.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClayton2006195–197-13)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20132-14)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202319-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Arkesilas_Painter_-_Arkesilas_supervising_the_silphion_trade_-_Paris_BnF_CabMed_189_-_02.jpg/250px-Arkesilas_Painter_-_Arkesilas_supervising_the_silphion_trade_-_Paris_BnF_CabMed_189_-_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arkesilas_Painter_-_Arkesilas_supervising_the_silphion_trade_-_Paris_BnF_CabMed_189_-_02.jpg)

[Arcesilaus II](/wiki/Arcesilaus_II_of_Cyrene "Arcesilaus II of Cyrene") oversees the weighing of [silphium](/wiki/Silphium "Silphium") for export, on a Laconian [kylix](/wiki/Kylix "Kylix"), c. 565–560 BC.

According to Herodotus, conflict with king [Arcesilaus II](/wiki/Arcesilaus_II_of_Cyrene "Arcesilaus II of Cyrene") "the Cruel" (c. 560–550 BC) led his brothers to leave the city and found the city of [Barca](/wiki/Barca_(ancient_city) "Barca (ancient city)") to the west. Archaeological evidence shows that Greek presence at Barca predates this foundation, going back to the seventh century.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick201368-15) Arcesilaus was defeated by the Barcans and Libyans at the Battle of Leuco, killed by his brother and succeeded by his infant son [Battus III](/wiki/Battus_III "Battus III") (c. 550–530 BC), under whom Cyrene continued to suffer from continued internal conflict.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202320-16) This was resolved through a reform of the city's laws by [Demonax](/wiki/Demonax_(lawmaker) "Demonax (lawmaker)") of [Mantinea](/wiki/Mantinea "Mantinea").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20133-17) These reforms appear to have limited the authority of the king to religious matters, vested political power in the Cyrenaean people, and divided the Cyreneans into three [tribes](/wiki/Phyle "Phyle"). He may also have mediated a peace with Barca and introduced [trial by combat](/wiki/Trial_by_combat "Trial by combat").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202320-16)

Battus III's son [Arcesilaus III](/wiki/Arcesilaus_III_of_Cyrene "Arcesilaus III of Cyrene") (c. 530–515 BC) attempted to revoke Demonax's constitution and was driven into exile. He returned with an army from [Samos](/wiki/Samos "Samos") and regained control but forced out once more and was assassinated at Barca. His mother [Pheretime](/wiki/Pheretime "Pheretime") appealed to the [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid "Achaemenid") governor of Egypt, [Aryandes](/wiki/Aryandes "Aryandes"), who besieged and sacked Barca in 515 BC. According to Herodotus, Aryandes marched his troops through Cyrene and then, regretting that he had not taken the opportunity to conquer Cyrene, attempted to get back in, but was prevented. The story is strange; it may be that the city was actually conquered by the Persians.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20133-17)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202321-12)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Remains of an extramural temple destroyed by the Persians at this time have been found.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013242-19)

### Classical period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Classical period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Temple_of_Zeus_-_Cyrene.jpg/250px-Temple_of_Zeus_-_Cyrene.jpg)](/wiki/File:Temple_of_Zeus_-_Cyrene.jpg)

The Temple of Zeus, Cyrene

In the fifth century BC, perhaps as a consequence of the Persian intervention, Cyrene's influence over the other Greek cities in Cyrenaica seems to have solidified into institutionalised political control.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202321-12) The city was prosperous and construction of the Temple of Apollo, [Temple of Zeus](/wiki/Temple_of_Zeus,_Cyrene "Temple of Zeus, Cyrene"), [Temple of Demeter](/wiki/Temple_of_Demeter,_Cyrene "Temple of Demeter, Cyrene"), and structures in the Agora date to this time.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202321-12) Cyrene's chief local export through much of its early history was the medicinal herb [silphium](/wiki/Silphium_(antiquity) "Silphium (antiquity)"), which may have been used as an [abortifacient](/wiki/Abortifacient "Abortifacient"); the herb was pictured on most Cyrenian coins. Silphium was in such demand that it was harvested to extinction by the end of the first century AD.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Cyrene also made money from raising of horses and the transhipment trade between Egypt, the Aegean, and [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage"). It was a landing point for Greeks seeking to visit the oracle of [Ammon](/wiki/Ammon "Ammon") at [Siwah](/wiki/Siwah "Siwah").[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20134-21)

[Arcesilaus IV](/wiki/Arcesilaus_IV "Arcesilaus IV") won the chariot race at the [Pythian Games](/wiki/Pythian_Games "Pythian Games") in 462 BC and at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Ancient_Olympics "Ancient Olympics") in 460 BC, in celebration of which Pindar wrote the *Fourth* and *Fifth Pythian Odes*. Following this victory, he organised a new wave of Greek settlement at Euesperides. Some time after this however, the Cyreneans monarchy was abolished in obscure circumstances and the tomb of his ancestor Battus I was destroyed.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20133_&_39-22)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202322-23) In 454 BC, Cyrene gave refuge to the remnants of an [Athenian](/wiki/Ancient_Athens "Ancient Athens") army that had been [defeated by the Persians in Egypt](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Delian_League#Egypt "Wars of the Delian League").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202322-23) In the following years, Barca seems to have become the dominant city in the region[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202323-24) and Cyrene was regularly in conflict with the other Greek cities of Cyrenaica and with the Libyans.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20133-17) In 414 BC, during the [Peloponnesian War](/wiki/Peloponnesian_War "Peloponnesian War"), [Spartan forces](/wiki/Spartan_army "Spartan army") travelling to [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") were driven to Cyrenaica by adverse winds and Cyrene provided them with two [triremes](/wiki/Trireme "Trireme") and [pilots](/wiki/Maritime_pilot "Maritime pilot") to lead them to Sicily.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20133-17)

Towards the end of the fifth century BC, one Ariston took control of the city, put five hundred leading Cyreneans to death and exiled others. It is possible that he attempted to establish a radical [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") on the Athenian model. A group of 3,000 [Messenians](/wiki/Messenia "Messenia") who had been expelled from [Naupactus](/wiki/Naupactus "Naupactus") by the Spartans arrived in Cyrene in 404 BC and joined forces with the exiles, but were almost all killed in a battle, after which the Cyrenean exiles and the followers of Ariston reconciled. The surviving Messenians settled at Euhesperides.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202323–24-26) There are some signs that civic conflict continued over the following century.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202325-27)

During the fourth century BC, Cyrene clashed with [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") over the [Syrtis](/wiki/Gulf_of_Sidra "Gulf of Sidra") and the trans-Saharan trade routes that ended there. The border was established at the [Altars of the Phileni](/w/index.php?title=Altars_of_the_Phileni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Altars of the Phileni (page does not exist)"). Cyrene may also have extended its control eastwards to [Catabathmus Magnus](/wiki/Sallum "Sallum"). Cyrene constructed a [treasury](/w/index.php?title=Treasury_of_the_Cyreneans&action=edit&redlink=1 "Treasury of the Cyreneans (page does not exist)") at Delphi between 350 and 325 BC.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202325-27) When [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") conquered Egypt in 331 BC and marched west to visit the oracle at [Siwah](/wiki/Siwah "Siwah"), the Cyreneans sent an embassy to declare their friendship; they did not come under Macedonian control. An inscription records that during a famine in the late 320s, Cyrene sent over 800,000 [medimni](/wiki/Medimni "Medimni") of grain (c. 40,000,000 [litres](/wiki/Litre "Litre")) to the cities of Greece and the Macedonian royal family.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Hellenistic period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hellenistic period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/British_Museum_%2815326027445%29.jpg/250px-British_Museum_%2815326027445%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Museum_(15326027445).jpg)

The Cyrene bronze head in the British Museum (300 BC)

In 324 BC, a Spartan mercenary leader, [Thibron](/wiki/Thibron_(mercenary_commander,_died_322_BC) "Thibron (mercenary commander, died 322 BC)"), joined forces with Cyrenean and Barcan exiles on Crete and invaded Cyrenaica, capturing Cyrene's port and forcing Cyrene to accept his rule.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202325-27) However, one of his officers, Mnasicles, defected to the Cyreneans and helped them to expel Thibron's troops and recapture the port.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202326-29) Cyrene allied with the Libyans and Carthaginians, but Thibron returned in 322 BC and defeated them. A democratic revolution took place in Cyrene and the exiled aristocrats appealed to [Ptolemy I Soter](/wiki/Ptolemy_I_Soter "Ptolemy I Soter") for help. Ptolemy sent his general [Ophellas](/wiki/Ophellas "Ophellas") to occupy the city and established a new constitution for the city, which is recorded on a large inscription,[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) which was heavily oligarchic and reserved a permanent role for himself in the city's administration.[[32]](#cite_note-AGA-32)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202326-29) The city was accepted by the other Macedonian leaders as part of the [Ptolemaic realm](/wiki/Ptolemaic_kingdom "Ptolemaic kingdom") in the [Treaty of Triparadisus](/wiki/Treaty_of_Triparadisus "Treaty of Triparadisus") in 321 BC. Cyrenean rebels attempted to expel the Ptolemaic garrison in 313 BC, but Ptolemy sent reinforcements who suppressed the revolt.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202326-29) In 308 BC, Ophellas led Cyrenaean and Athenian troops west to join [Agathocles of Syracuse](/wiki/Agathocles_of_Syracuse "Agathocles of Syracuse")'s attack on Carthage and was immediately murdered.[[32]](#cite_note-AGA-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Magas_as_king_of_Kyrene%2C_circa_282_or_275_to_261_BC.jpg/250px-Magas_as_king_of_Kyrene%2C_circa_282_or_275_to_261_BC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Magas_as_king_of_Kyrene,_circa_282_or_275_to_261_BC.jpg)

Coin of [Magas](/wiki/Magas_of_Cyrene "Magas of Cyrene") as king of Cyrene, circa 282/75 to 261 BC

Cyrene rebelled against Ptolemy again around 305 BC. Control was re-established in 300 BC by Ptolemy's step-son [Magas](/wiki/Magas_of_Cyrene "Magas of Cyrene").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202327-33) After Ptolemy's death in 282 BC, Magas refused to submit to his half-brother [Ptolemy II](/wiki/Ptolemy_II "Ptolemy II") and had crowned himself king by 276 BC. He married [Apama](/wiki/Apama_II "Apama II") the daughter of the [Seleucid](/wiki/Seleucid_Empire "Seleucid Empire") king [Antiochus I](/wiki/Antiochus_I "Antiochus I") and assisted him in an unsuccessful invasion of Egypt during the [First Syrian War](/wiki/First_Syrian_War "First Syrian War").[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202327-33) Inscribed accounts indicate severe [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") of food prices and a large fundraising campaign, possibly for repairs to the city walls.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202327-33) After his death, Apama invited a Macedonian prince, [Demetrius the Fair](/wiki/Demetrius_the_Fair "Demetrius the Fair"), to marry her daughter [Berenice](/wiki/Berenice_II_of_Egypt "Berenice II of Egypt") and take the throne, but he was murdered after a short conflict with Berenice. She married [Ptolemy III](/wiki/Ptolemy_III "Ptolemy III") in 246 BC, bringing Cyrene back under Ptolemaic control.[[35]](#cite_note-35) In the process, the city of Euesperides was destroyed and re-founded as Berenice and the cities of Cyrenaica formed a federation, called the Pentapolis, which minted its own coinage.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20135-36) Constitutional reforms by a pair of [Arcadians](/wiki/Arcadia_(region) "Arcadia (region)"), Ecdelus and Demophanes, may also belong in this period.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202328-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Cyrene_%2845%29_%288288433101%29.jpg/250px-Cyrene_%2845%29_%288288433101%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cyrene_(45)_(8288433101).jpg)

[Gymnasium](/w/index.php?title=Caesareum,_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Caesareum, Cyrene (page does not exist)") of Cyrene

Cyrene was reduced to subject status, a garrison was installed, and a succession of Ptolemaic courtiers were appointed to the city's [priesthood of Apollo](/wiki/Priest_of_Apollo_(Cyrene) "Priest of Apollo (Cyrene)").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202328-37) Cyrene was established as a separate kingdom once more for [Ptolemy VIII](/wiki/Ptolemy_VIII "Ptolemy VIII") in 163 BC after his siblings expelled him from Egypt.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrainger2010325,_327-38)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHölbl2001185–186-39)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202329-40) The city rebelled against him but was defeated. It is possible that he granted Cyrene's port, Apollonia, independence from Cyrene at this time, as a reward for remaining loyal.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013259-41) Ptolemy engaged in a wide-ranging construction project in the city, including the construction of a monumental [gymnasium](/w/index.php?title=Caesareum,_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Caesareum, Cyrene (page does not exist)").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202329-40) He also had a will inscribed, promising Cyrene to the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") in the event that he died without heirs. However, he regained control of Egypt in 145 BC.[[42]](#cite_note-42) In the dynastic conflicts that followed, Cyrene probably remained under the control of Ptolemy VIII and then of [Ptolemy IX](/wiki/Ptolemy_IX "Ptolemy IX").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202329-40) It was apparently given to Ptolemy VIII's illegitimate son [Ptolemy Apion](/wiki/Ptolemy_Apion "Ptolemy Apion") as a separate kingdom c. 105–101 BC. Apion made a similar will to that of his father and the territory passed to Rome when he died without heirs in 96 BC.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20135-36)

The city became an important Jewish centre during the Hellenistic period. The [deuterocanonical](/wiki/Deuterocanonical_books "Deuterocanonical books") book [2 Maccabees](/wiki/2_Maccabees "2 Maccabees") is said by its author to be an abridgment of a five-volume work by a Hellenized Jew by the name of [Jason of Cyrene](/wiki/Jason_of_Cyrene "Jason of Cyrene") who lived around 100 BC.

### Roman period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Roman period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Marble_bust_of_Emperor_Antoninus_Pius._138-161_CE._From_the_house_of_Jason_Magnus_at_Cyrene%2C_modern-day_Libya._The_British_Museum%2C_London.jpg/250px-Marble_bust_of_Emperor_Antoninus_Pius._138-161_CE._From_the_house_of_Jason_Magnus_at_Cyrene%2C_modern-day_Libya._The_British_Museum%2C_London.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marble_bust_of_Emperor_Antoninus_Pius._138-161_CE._From_the_house_of_Jason_Magnus_at_Cyrene,_modern-day_Libya._The_British_Museum,_London.jpg)

Marble bust of Emperor Antoninus Pius (r. 138–161 AD), from the [house of Jason Magnus](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Jason_Magnus&action=edit&redlink=1 "House of Jason Magnus (page does not exist)") at Cyrene, now in The British Museum, London

After 96 BC, the Romans initially ignored the new territory. [Plutarch](/wiki/Plutarch "Plutarch") mentions a tyrant of Cyrene, [Nicocrates](/w/index.php?title=Nicocrates&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nicocrates (page does not exist)"), who was deposed by his wife [Aretaphila of Cyrene](/wiki/Aretaphila_of_Cyrene "Aretaphila of Cyrene") and succeeded by his brother Learchus, who was murdered in turn.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202330-44) [Lucullus](/wiki/Lucullus "Lucullus") visited the city in 87 BC, suppressed the tyranny and granted Cyrene a new constitution.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosamilia202330-44) But it was only in 74 BC that the Romans first sent a governor, Publius Cornelius Lentulus Marcellinus.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20135-36) At some point between 67 and 30 BC, Cyrenaica became part of the [Roman province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province") of [Crete and Cyrenaica](/wiki/Crete_and_Cyrenaica "Crete and Cyrenaica"). The provincial capital was on Crete, but Cyrene remained the chief city in Cyrenaica and enjoyed a highly prosperous period and much construction dates to the first century AD.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20135-36) In the mid-first century AD, the Roman authorities launched an extensive surveying campaign to reclaim the public land around Cyrene that had slipped into private control and stopped paying dividends to the [fisc](/wiki/Fisc "Fisc").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20136-45)

Because of its large Jewish population, Cyrene was an [early centre of Christianity](/wiki/Early_centers_of_Christianity#Cyrene "Early centers of Christianity"). A Cyrenian named [Simon](/wiki/Simon_of_Cyrene "Simon of Cyrene") carried the cross of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ").[[46]](#cite_note-46) [Acts](/wiki/Acts_of_the_Apostles "Acts of the Apostles") claims that Jews from Cyrene heard the disciples speaking in their own language in Jerusalem on the day of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") and later says that Jewish Christians from Cyrene and Cyprus were among those in Antioch who began spreading the gospel among Gentiles.[[47]](#cite_note-47) According to the tradition of the [Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church"), its founder, [Saint Mark](/wiki/Saint_Mark "Saint Mark") was a native of Cyrene and ordained the first bishop of Cyrene.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Apollo_Kitharoidos_BM_1380.jpg/250px-Apollo_Kitharoidos_BM_1380.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apollo_Kitharoidos_BM_1380.jpg)

[Apollo Kitharoidos](/wiki/Apollo_of_Cyrene "Apollo of Cyrene") from Cyrene. Roman statue from the second century AD now in the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum")

A massive Jewish revolt, the [Diaspora revolt](/wiki/Diaspora_revolt "Diaspora revolt"), broke out in Cyrenaica, Egypt, Mesopotamia, Cyprus and Judaea in 115 AD. Cyrene was sacked and almost all of the city's buildings were destroyed. Literary sources claim that 220,000 people were killed before the revolt was quelled by [Marcius Turbo](/wiki/Marcius_Turbo "Marcius Turbo").[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013149-49) According to [Eusebius of Caesarea](/wiki/Eusebius_of_Caesarea "Eusebius of Caesarea"), the Jewish rebellion left Libya depopulated to such an extent that a few years later new colonies had to be established there by the emperor [Hadrian](/wiki/Hadrian "Hadrian") to maintain the viability of continued settlement. Restoration work is recorded in inscriptions and visible archaeologically; it was not completed until the reign of [Commodus](/wiki/Commodus "Commodus").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20136-45) The city was an early member of Hadrian's [Panhellenion](/wiki/Panhellenion "Panhellenion") and a long inscription records its attempts to block membership for one of its neighbours. Cyrene was once again prosperous by the third quarter of the second century AD and several palaces date to this period, including the [House of Jason Magnus](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Jason_Magnus&action=edit&redlink=1 "House of Jason Magnus (page does not exist)").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20136,_163-50)

In the mid-third century AD, Cyrene's economy began to decline. This was hastened by an [earthquake](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") of 262, which destroyed much of the city.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20136-45) After the disaster, the city was raided by the [Marmaritae](/wiki/Marmarica "Marmarica"), Libyan nomads, who were defeated in 269 by [Tenagino Probus](/wiki/Tenagino_Probus "Tenagino Probus"), prefect of Egypt. The emperor [Claudius Gothicus](/wiki/Claudius_II "Claudius II") restored Cyrene, naming it *Claudiopolis*. Many buildings were subsequently rebuilt,[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20137-51) but a hurriedly built new defensive wall enclosed only the western half of the city.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013149-49) The civic hub shifted north from the street of Battus to the Valley street and many of the old public spaces were filled in with housing and shops.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013149,_154-52) In the reforms of [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian"), Cyrene became part of the new province of [Libya Superior](/wiki/Libya_Superior "Libya Superior") (also called Pentapolis).[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20136-45) The [Roman Martyrology](/wiki/Roman_Martyrology "Roman Martyrology")[[53]](#cite_note-53) mentions under 4 July a tradition that in the [persecution of Diocletian](/wiki/Persecution_of_Diocletian "Persecution of Diocletian") a bishop Theodorus of Cyrene was scourged and had his tongue cut out. Earlier editions of the Martyrology mentioned what may be the same person also under 26 March.

### Byzantine period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Byzantine period")]

Another [earthquake](/wiki/365_Crete_earthquake "365 Crete earthquake") destroyed the city on 21 July 365. Skeletons crushed by falling masonry have been found and one tomb inscription mentions the earthquake.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20137-51) A contemporary historian, [Ammianus Marcellinus](/wiki/Ammianus_Marcellinus "Ammianus Marcellinus"), describes Cyrene as "an ancient but deserted city".[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20137-51) However, the damage may have been over-emphasised. Archaeology shows that most buildings were damaged, but also that many were rebuilt, including many temples, which were only closed by the [Theodosian decrees](/wiki/Persecution_of_pagans_under_Theodosius_I "Persecution of pagans under Theodosius I") in 395.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20137,_150-54) Settlement seems to have expanded east beyond Claudius Gothicus' fortification wall and a generation after the earthquake, Cyrene was a significant centre.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013149-49) [Synesius](/wiki/Synesius "Synesius"), a wealthy magnate who became bishop of Ptolemais and whose letters are preserved, grew up in Cyrene in the generation after the earthquake.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick20138-55) Letter 67 of Synesius tells of an irregular episcopal ordination carried out by a bishop Philo of Cyrene, which was condoned by [Athanasius](/wiki/Athanasius "Athanasius"). The same letter mentions that a nephew of this Philo, who bore the same name, also became bishop of Cyrene.

The [Central](/w/index.php?title=Central_Church,_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Central Church, Cyrene (page does not exist)") and [East Churches](/w/index.php?title=East_Church,_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "East Church, Cyrene (page does not exist)") were built in the fifth or sixth century AD and renovated several times.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013150,_188,_225–226-56) A bishop of Cyrene name Rufus attended the [Robber Council of Ephesus](/wiki/Robber_Council_of_Ephesus "Robber Council of Ephesus") in 449 and there was still a bishop of Cyrene, named Leontius, at the time of [Patriarch](/wiki/List_of_Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchs_of_Alexandria "List of Greek Orthodox Patriarchs of Alexandria") [Eulogius of Alexandria](/wiki/Eulogius_of_Alexandria "Eulogius of Alexandria") (580–607).[[57]](#cite_note-Michel_Le_Quien-57)[[58]](#cite_note-Janin-58) The city fell under [Arab conquest](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Maghreb "Muslim conquest of the Maghreb") in 643. At some point thereafter it was abandoned, but the ancient name lived on as "Grennah" in the 19th century.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013151-59)

### Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Modern history")]

The site was totally abandoned in the early modern period. [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick_William_Beechey "Frederick William Beechey") and [Richard Beechey](/wiki/Richard_Brydges_Beechey "Richard Brydges Beechey") visited and produced the first site plans in 1821–1822. The French consul at Benghazi looted part of a tomb later in the century for the [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre"). The first systematic excavations were undertaken by [Robert Murdoch Smith](/wiki/Robert_Murdoch_Smith "Robert Murdoch Smith") and E. A. Porcher in 1860 and 1861; their findings mostly went to the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum").[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick201315-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) They include the Apollo of Cyrene and a unique bronze head of an African man.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In 1909, the [Jewish Territorial Organization](/wiki/Jewish_Territorial_Organization "Jewish Territorial Organization"), a newly formed [Zionist](/wiki/Zionist "Zionist") group also known as the ITO, led by [Israel Zangwill](/wiki/Israel_Zangwill "Israel Zangwill"), briefly considered the region known as Cyrenaica as the possible site for a Jewish homeland. But when it was discovered that the region was basically "waterless" and that even irrigation would be impossible, the desperately hopeful plans were immediately abandoned.[[64]](#cite_note-Cockerell-64)

The American [Richard Norton](/wiki/Richard_Norton_(archaeologist) "Richard Norton (archaeologist)") began more scientific excavations in 1910, which were halted by the [Italian invasion of Libya](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Libya "Italian invasion of Libya") in 1911.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick201315-60) The tomb of the excavation's epigrapher, Herbert de Cou, who was shot in mysterious circumstances, is located on the site.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013237-65)

The Italian colonial government established a military base at the site in 1913.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013151-59) In the course of building the base, Italian soldiers found the "[Venus of Cyrene](/w/index.php?title=Venus_of_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Venus of Cyrene (page does not exist)")", a headless marble statue representing the goddess Venus, a Roman copy of a Greek original, which prompted them to restrict their base to the Acropolis. The statue was transported to Rome, where it remained until 2008, when it was returned to Libya.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The village of Shahat grew up on the site as a result of the Italian presence.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013151-59)

The Italians created an antiquities service and, after the discovery of the Venus of Cyrene, carried out excavations at Cyrene on a very large scale, which were closely connected with the regime's propaganda. The Italian archaeologists were expelled in 1943 when the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") captured Cyrenaica.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick201315-60) [Richard Goodchild](/wiki/Richard_Goodchild "Richard Goodchild"), controller of antiquities from 1955 to 1966 moved the village of Shahat off the site and re-established it to the south; it has since expanded over much of the southern necropolis.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013151-59) He also restored control of excavations at the site to the Italians, under [Sandro Stucchi](/w/index.php?title=Sandro_Stucchi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sandro Stucchi (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sandro_Stucchi "it:Sandro Stucchi")]. Goodchild also The Italian mission has excavated much of the site and restored several buildings through the process of [anastylosis](/wiki/Anastylosis "Anastylosis").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick201316-67)

The site was declared a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 1982.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Beginning in 2006, the [Global Heritage Fund](/wiki/Global_Heritage_Fund "Global Heritage Fund"), in partnership with the [Second University of Naples](/wiki/Second_University_of_Naples "Second University of Naples") (SUN, Italy), the [Libyan](/wiki/Libya "Libya") Department of Antiquities, and the Libyan Ministry of Culture, worked to preserve the ancient site through a combination of holistic conservation practices and training of local skilled and unskilled labor. The [GHF](/wiki/Global_Heritage_Fund "Global Heritage Fund")-led team conducted ongoing emergency conservation on the [theater](/w/index.php?title=Greek_theatre_of_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Greek theatre of Cyrene (page does not exist)") inside the [Sanctuary of Apollo](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Apollo "Sanctuary of Apollo").[[69]](#cite_note-69)

In 2017 [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") added Cyrene to its [List of World Heritage in Danger](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_in_Danger "List of World Heritage in Danger").[[70]](#cite_note-70)

## Archaeological site

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Archaeological site")]

Cyrene is now an archaeological site north of the village of [Shahhat](/wiki/Shahhat "Shahhat") and east of [Bayda](/wiki/Bayda,_Libya "Bayda, Libya"), on a ridge of the Jabal Akhdar, about 600 metres above sea level. The southern edge of the ridge and the city is formed by the Wadi Bil Ghadir and the northern edge by the Wadi Bu Turqiyah. The Acropolis, at the western edge of the ridge, was the original centre of Greek occupation. From there, a road referred to by modern scholars as the "Street of Battus" or "Skyrotà" runs along the ridge to the southeast for around 1 kilometre, past the [Agora](/w/index.php?title=Agora_of_Ancient_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Agora of Ancient Cyrene (page does not exist)"), the [House of Jason Magnus](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Jason_Magnus&action=edit&redlink=1 "House of Jason Magnus (page does not exist)") and a number of other palatial residences, the [Stoa of Hermes and Heracles](/w/index.php?title=Stoa_of_Hermes_and_Heracles&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stoa of Hermes and Heracles (page does not exist)"), the [Caesareum](/w/index.php?title=Caesareum,_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Caesareum, Cyrene (page does not exist)"), two theatres, a sacred area, and the [caravanserai](/w/index.php?title=Caravanserai,_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Caravanserai, Cyrene (page does not exist)") until it reaches the gates of the city. Below the Acropolis to the north, the Springs of Apollo and Cyra emerge from the cliff-face onto a triangular plateau at the base of the Wadi Bu Turqiyah. This plateau contains the [Greek Theatre](/wiki/Greek_Theatre_of_Cyrene "Greek Theatre of Cyrene"), the [Sanctuary of Apollo](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Apollo,_Cyrene "Sanctuary of Apollo, Cyrene"), and the [Baths of Trajan](/w/index.php?title=Baths_of_Trajan,_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Baths of Trajan, Cyrene (page does not exist)"). From the sanctuary, a road known as "Valley Street" leads southeast up the Wadi Bu Turqiyah, roughly parallel to the "Street of Battus", lined by a stepped portico and the [Aqua Augusta](/w/index.php?title=Aqua_Augusta,_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Aqua Augusta, Cyrene (page does not exist)"), past the [Baths of Paris](/w/index.php?title=Baths_of_Paris&action=edit&redlink=1 "Baths of Paris (page does not exist)") to the [Market Theatre](/w/index.php?title=Market_Theatre_(Cyrene)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Market Theatre (Cyrene) (page does not exist)") and the [Central Quarter](/w/index.php?title=Central_Quarter,_Cyrene&action=edit&redlink=1 "Central Quarter, Cyrene (page does not exist)"), which contains several public buildings and palatial residences. To the northeast, on another ridge, but still inside the city walls, is the largely unexcavated northeastern quarter, containing the Temple of Zeus, the hippodrome, and the East Church. Outside the city walls to the south is the [Sanctuary of Demeter and Persephone](/wiki/Extramural_Sanctuary_of_Demeter_and_Persephone,_Cyrene "Extramural Sanctuary of Demeter and Persephone, Cyrene"). The [necropolis of Cyrene](/wiki/Necropolis_of_Cyrene "Necropolis of Cyrene") covers about 20 km² to the south and north of the city.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013148–254-71)

Archaeological finds are stored and displayed in a temporary museum in the eastern portion of the site. In 2005, Italian archaeologists from the [University of Urbino](/wiki/University_of_Urbino "University of Urbino") discovered 76 intact Roman statues at Cyrene from the 2nd century AD. The statues remained undiscovered for so long because "during the earthquake of 375 AD, a supporting wall of the temple fell on its side, burying all the statues. They remained hidden under stone, rubble and earth for 1,630 years. The other walls sheltered the statues, so we were able to recover all the pieces, even works that had been broken."[[72]](#cite_note-72)

### Acropolis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Acropolis")]

The [Acropolis](/wiki/Acropolis "Acropolis") lies to the West of the [Agora](/wiki/Agora "Agora") and South of the Sanctuary of Apollo at the Western end of the Route of Skyrota also known as the Street of Battus.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The acropolis was the location of the Royal Palace and the source of the sacred spring, known as the Fountain of Apollo, which ran through the sanctuary of Apollo to the North.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### Agora

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Agora")]

The Agora lies in the centre of the city with an Eastern and Western [Stoa](/wiki/Stoa "Stoa"), as well as several temples such as the Temple of Apollo Archegetes.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### Sanctuary of Apollo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Sanctuary of Apollo")]

One of its more significant features is the [temple](/wiki/Greek_temple "Greek temple") of [Apollo](/wiki/Apollo "Apollo"), which was originally constructed as early as 7th century BC. Other ancient structures include a temple to [Demeter](/wiki/Demeter "Demeter"). There is a large [necropolis](/wiki/Necropolis "Necropolis") approximately 10 km between Cyrene and its ancient port of Apollonia.

### Temple of Zeus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Temple of Zeus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Cyrene_%281%29_%288289544346%29.jpg/250px-Cyrene_%281%29_%288289544346%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cyrene_(1)_(8289544346).jpg)

The reconstructed temple of Zeus, seen from the southwest.

Main article: [Temple of Zeus, Cyrene](/wiki/Temple_of_Zeus,_Cyrene "Temple of Zeus, Cyrene")

The **Temple of Zeus** was the largest ancient Greek temple at Cyrene, and one of the largest Greek temples ever built. The original [Doric](/wiki/Doric_order "Doric order") [octastyle](/wiki/Octastyle "Octastyle") [peripteral](/wiki/Peripteral "Peripteral") temple was constructed around 500–480 BC,[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013222-76) It faced east and stood atop a three-stepped [crepidoma](/wiki/Crepidoma "Crepidoma"), with a length of 68.3 metres and a width of 30.4 metres,[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDinsmoor195086-77) making it roughly the same size as the [Temple of Zeus](/wiki/Temple_of_Zeus,_Olympia "Temple of Zeus, Olympia") at [Olympia](/wiki/Ancient_Olympia "Ancient Olympia") and the [Parthenon](/wiki/Parthenon "Parthenon") at [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens").[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013222-76) The front porch ([pronaos](/wiki/Pronaos "Pronaos")) was supported by two columns [in antis](/wiki/In_antis "In antis"); the back porch ([opisthodomos](/wiki/Opisthodomos "Opisthodomos")) by three columns in antis. The [cella](/wiki/Cella "Cella") was two stories high and two rows of columns divided it into three aisles. The external colonnade ([peripteros](/wiki/Peripteros "Peripteros")) has eight columns at the front and rear and seventeen columns on each of the long sides. It was destroyed in 115 AD during the Jewish sack of the city. Around 172–175 AD it was partially rebuilt as a non-peripteral temple. Between 185 and 192 AD, a colossal cult statue, modelled on the [Statue of Zeus at Olympia](/wiki/Statue_of_Zeus_at_Olympia "Statue of Zeus at Olympia") was installed.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodchildReynoldsHerington195851-61-78) The temple was destroyed once more in 365 AD by an earthquake and then burnt by Christians.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodchildReynoldsHerington195839-79)

### Sanctuary of Demeter and Persephone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sanctuary of Demeter and Persephone")]

Main article: [Extramural Sanctuary of Demeter and Persephone, Cyrene](/wiki/Extramural_Sanctuary_of_Demeter_and_Persephone,_Cyrene "Extramural Sanctuary of Demeter and Persephone, Cyrene")

The sanctuary to Demeter and Persephone, which includes a [temple](/wiki/Temple "Temple") and theater complex, is located south of the Wadi Bil Ghadir ravine, outside the city walls. The Sanctuary comprised structures sprawled out over twenty miles and are divided into three terraces: the Lower, Middle and Upper Sanctuaries.[[80]](#cite_note-80) The archaeological remains date from the late seventh century BC to the mid-third century AD. During the time of this sacred activity at the Sanctuary a voluminous amount of [votive](/wiki/Votive "Votive") material was accumulated in its interior: pottery, lamps, coinage, stone sculpture, jewellery, inscriptions, glass, as well as bronze and [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") [figurines](/wiki/Figurine "Figurine"). The pottery excavated at the Sanctuary provides useful evidence concerning both the question of its foundation and type of religious activity.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### Necropolis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Necropolis")]

Main article: [Necropolis of Cyrene](/wiki/Necropolis_of_Cyrene "Necropolis of Cyrene")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Cyrene_%2859%29_%288289473930%29.jpg/250px-Cyrene_%2859%29_%288289473930%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cyrene_(59)_(8289473930).jpg)

Rock-cut tombs in the [necropolis of Cyrene](/wiki/Necropolis_of_Cyrene "Necropolis of Cyrene").

The necropolis consists of graves, rock-cut tombs, temple-tombs, and sarcophagi, dating from the sixth century BC until the fifth century AD. It covers about 20 km² to the south and north of the city, making it one of the largest known Greek necropoleis.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013235-82) The southern section has been encroached upon by the growing city of Shahat, especially after 2013, when many tombs were bulldozed.[[83]](#cite_note-ANN2013-83) The northern portion is better preserved. Several of the tombs of the Roman period have niches for portrait busts of the deceased. A common find are statues of the so-called "Goddess of Death", a female bust – often faceless – depicted in the process of unveiling herself.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenrick2013238,_244–245-84)

### Water system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Water system")]

In October 2023, a flood exposed a water canal, possibly dating back to the Roman era.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

## Philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cyrene,_Libya&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Philosophy")]

Cyrene contributed to the intellectual life of the Greeks, through renowned philosophers and mathematicians. The School of Cyrene, known as the [Cyrenaics](/wiki/Cyrenaics "Cyrenaics"), developed here as a minor [Socratic](/wiki/Socratic_method "Socratic method") school founded by [Aristippus](/wiki/Aristippus "Aristippus") (perhaps the friend of [Socrates](/wiki/Socrates "Socrates"), though according to some accounts a grandson of Aristippus with the same name).

* Filo II (fl.370 circa)
* Rufo (fl.449)
* Leontius (fl.600 circa)

No longer a residential bishopric, Cyrene is today listed by the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") as a [titular see](/wiki/Titular_see "Titular see").[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) The [Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church") has also treated it as a titular see.[[58]](#cite_note-Janin-58)
